blocks.content: On the sidewalk, the night was mild, the kind of May evening that had not yet decided to become full summer but was offering a preview. The group negotiated the logistics of tomorrow — who was driving, what time, the particular debate about eleven versus noon that Nathan resolved by saying noon firmly and then moving on before anyone could object.
Ethan came to stand beside Cassandra while the others were finding keys and determining ride-shares. He said it quietly, not urgently:

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I think I want to go.
It was a slightly larger answer than his question. He heard that. He didn't follow it with anything — just nodded once, the nod of someone who received it correctly.

Lena was already in her car. Nathan and Maya were having a conversation about something that had dissolved into laughter. Ethan looked at Cassandra for a moment longer. She was looking at the Copper Mug window — at Mr. Alvarez inside, still moving through his quiet routines, the warm light of the place surrounding him like it always did.

She went to Lena's car. Ethan watched her go, then watched the coffee shop window for a moment, then walked to his own car. He drove home past Fairfield Road. The cherry trees were lit by streetlamps — pale pink, almost luminous in the dark. He slowed the car without quite meaning to.
He sat there for a moment at the side of the road, looking at them.
